subject: Statistics
title: Hazard Ratios and Survival Curves
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Hazard Ratios and Survival Curves.

hook.text: A cancer drug has a hazard ratio of 0.7. Patients hear 'reduces death by 30%.' What does it really mean?

buildup.text: A hazard ratio compares the rate of events over time between two groups. Below 1 means the treatment is better.

discovery.text: Kaplan-Meier survival curves show the probability of surviving over time — a visual story of the drug's impact.

twist.text: A hazard ratio of 0.7 doesn't mean 30% fewer deaths overall. It means 30% lower risk at any given moment.

climax.text: Understanding survival statistics is critical. Patients and doctors make life-or-death decisions based on these numbers.

punchline.text: Survival curves show the full story. A single number can't.

quiz.question: What does a hazard ratio of 0.7 mean?
options: At any given moment, treated patients have 30% lower risk of the event than controls
